title: GRADIENT DESCENT & OPTIMIZERS
sub: Visual exam summary — every section maps to a real quiz question.
source: Source · Tilburg — Introduction to Deep Learning · Lecture 3 (Gradient Descent, Optimizers, PyTorch & Hyperparameters)

sections.title: GRADIENT DESCENT & THE SIGN OF THE STEP
sections.exam: Goal is to MINIMIZE the cost J: the step is θ ← θ − α·∇J, so a positive gradient (prediction above target) forces a NEGATIVE step, and a learning rate that is too high overshoots the minimum and fails to converge.
sections.ascii:
GOAL: minimize cost J(θ)        update:  θ ← θ − α·(∂J/∂θ)
                                          └step┘    └ gradient ┘

example (MSE):  prediction ŷ  >  target y   (ŷ too big)
   ∂J/∂θ  = +     gradient is POSITIVE
   step   = −α·(+) = −     step is NEGATIVE  →  θ moves DOWN
                                               pulling ŷ back toward y

learning rate α = SIZE of the step:
   α too small ─▶ ·_·_·_·_   crawls, very slow
   α just right ▶ ╲________   smooth descent to the minimum
   α too LARGE ─▶ ╱╲╱╲╱╲╱╲   OVERSHOOTS — bounces, never converges

figures.label: WEB DIAGRAM
figures.desc: Each marked step (0,1,2,...) moves DOWN the gradient toward the centre of the bowl — the minimum of J. That downhill direction is why the step has the opposite sign to the gradient.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: W ← W − η·ΔW: parameters move AGAINST the gradient, scaled by the learning rate η. This is the exact mechanism behind the sign-of-the-step question.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: θ = θ − α·∇J(θ). Plain GD can get stuck in local minima, a good learning rate is hard to pick, and the SAME rate applies to every parameter — the gaps that optimizers later fix.

sections.title: MOMENTUM & NESTEROV LOOK-AHEAD
sections.exam: Momentum accelerates convergence by accumulating past gradients into a velocity (an exponentially weighted average); SGD-with-momentum and Nesterov differ ONLY in WHERE the gradient is computed — at the look-ahead position θ − β·v, not the current one.
sections.ascii:
MOMENTUM = a heavy ball with inertia: reuse past gradients (velocity v = EMA).
   v_t = β·v_(t-1) + (1−β)·∇J(θ)        θ ← θ − α·v_t
         └ remember the past ┘ └ new ┘     builds speed in steady directions,
                                            damps the noisy zig-zag

SGD+MOMENTUM   vs   NESTEROV (look-ahead):
   classic :  measure gradient HERE  ●───▶  then add momentum
   nesterov:  jump by momentum FIRST ●----▶◌  measure gradient THERE
                                            (at θ − β·v = the look-ahead point)

   ⇒ the ONLY difference = WHERE the gradient is evaluated
        current position   (SGD+momentum)   vs   look-ahead   (Nesterov)

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: v_t = β·v_(t-1) + (1−β)·x_t: a filtered, decaying average of past values. This EMA is the 'accumulate past gradients' engine inside momentum, RMSProp and Adam.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: θ = θ − α·v, with v = β·v_(t-1) + (1−β)·∇J. The contour plots show momentum (bottom) cutting the wild oscillation of plain GD (top) and reaching the minimum faster.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: The label 'Gradient is calculated here' sits at the LOOK-AHEAD point (θ − β·v) for Nesterov, but at the current point for standard momentum — the single distinction the exam tests.

sections.title: ADAPTIVE RATES — ADAGRAD vs RMSPROP
sections.exam: Adaptive methods give each parameter its own step by scaling α by the inverse square root of its past squared gradients; AdaGrad's RAW SUM grows forever and shrinks the rate toward zero, so RMSProp/Adam use an exponentially weighted moving average to keep the denominator bounded.
sections.ascii:
Adaptive LR = a PER-PARAMETER step, scaled by that param's own gradient size.
   step_i = α / (√(accumulator_i) + ε) · g_i      big grads → smaller steps

ADAGRAD:   r ← r + g²        RAW SUM — only ever grows
   denom √r ─▶ ▁▂▃▅▇█  keeps climbing → step → 0 → learning STALLS

RMSPROP / ADAM:   v ← β·v + (1−β)·g²     EXP. MOVING AVERAGE
   denom √v ─▶ ▃▄▃▄▃▄  stays BOUNDED → step stays usable  ✓ the fix

why preferred: the moving average stops the denominator from blowing up,
so the learning rate never collapses the way AdaGrad's does.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: r_t,i = r_(t-1),i + g²: the accumulator is a RAW SUM of squared gradients, so √r only grows and the per-parameter rate keeps shrinking — AdaGrad's weakness.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: v_t,i = β·v_(t-1),i + (1−β)·g²: the EXPONENTIALLY WEIGHTED AVERAGE replaces the raw sum, so √v stays bounded and the learning rate does not vanish — the answer to 'why moving average over raw sum'.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Trajectories of SGD, Momentum, NAG, Adagrad, Adadelta and RMSprop escaping a saddle point — adaptive methods adjust the step to the local curvature of the cost surface.

sections.title: ADAM — FIRST & SECOND MOMENTS
sections.exam: Adam tracks two per-parameter quantities: m = the FIRST moment (mean of the gradients) and v = the SECOND moment (uncentered variance, i.e. mean of g squared), with β1 and β2 their exponential decay rates — combining Momentum with RMSProp.
sections.ascii:
ADAM = Momentum (1st moment)  +  RMSProp (2nd moment), per parameter.

   m_t = β1·m_(t-1) + (1−β1)·g       ← 1st moment = MEAN of gradients
   v_t = β2·v_(t-1) + (1−β2)·g²      ← 2nd moment = uncentered VARIANCE
         β1, β2 = decay rates        g = gradient at step t

   update:   θ ← θ − α · m̂ / (√v̂ + ε)
              └ direction (m) ┘  └ adaptive size (v) ┘

   m gives the smoothed direction; √v shrinks the step where gradients are big.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: m_t,i = β1·m + (1−β1)·g and v_t,i = β2·v + (1−β2)·g²: the moving average of gradients (1st moment) and of squared gradients (2nd moment) — the exact interpretation the quiz asks for.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: θ = θ − α/(√v̂ + ε)·m̂. The division by √v̂ adapts the learning rate per parameter, scaling it inversely to the size of past squared gradients.

sections.title: ADAM — ONE STEP, BY HAND
sections.exam: First Adam step with θ0=0.5, g=0.2, α=0.1, β1=0.9, β2=0.999: m1=0.02, v1=0.00004, bias-corrected m̂=0.2 and v̂=0.04, denominator √v̂+ε≈0.20000001, update Δ≈0.1, so θ1=0.4.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: m̂ = m/(1−β1^t) and v̂ = v/(1−β2^t). The graph shows the corrected estimate (red) jumping up from the zero-biased start (green) — exactly the m̂=0.2, v̂=0.04 step in the worked example.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: θ = θ − α·m̂/(√v̂ + ε): substituting m̂=0.2, v̂=0.04, α=0.1 gives Δ≈0.1 and θ1=0.4 — the final blank in the calculation question.

sections.title: OPTIMIZER STATE — COUNTING SCALARS
sections.exam: Per iteration you store value + gradient + optimizer state: plain SGD keeps 2 scalars per parameter, Momentum/Nesterov 3, and Adam 4 (it adds m and v); always count parameters WITH biases, in×out + out per layer.
sections.ascii:
Per iteration store:  params + grads + optimizer state.
   params per layer = (in × out) + out      the +out is the BIAS — never drop it!

   scalars stored PER PARAMETER:
      SGD (plain) ........ 2   value + grad
      Momentum / Nesterov  3   value + grad + velocity
      Adam ............... 4   value + grad + m + v

 EXAM A:  MLP 8 → 5 → 3 → 2, biases on, Adam
   params = (8·5+5) + (5·3+3) + (3·2+2) = 45 + 18 + 8 = 71
   Adam → 4 × 71 = 284 scalars

 EXAM B:  MLP 5 → 4 → 3, biases on, plain SGD
   params = (5·4+4) + (4·3+3) = 24 + 15 = 39  →  2 × 39 = 78 scalars

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Validation accuracy of common optimizers over training — Adam-family methods climb fastest, the practical pay-off for storing two extra moment buffers per parameter.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: m and v are the extra per-parameter state on top of value and gradient — that is why Adam costs 4 scalars per parameter while plain SGD costs only 2.

sections.title: PYTORCH LOOP & HYPERPARAMETER SEARCH
sections.exam: The loop zeroes grads (PyTorch ACCUMULATES by default), forwards, computes CrossEntropyLoss on RAW logits, backprops, then optimizer.step updates ALL weights+biases under torch.no_grad; grid search is exhaustive, random search covers more in fewer trials, Bayesian reuses past results.
sections.ascii:
TRAIN LOOP (each mini-batch):
   optimizer.zero_grad()  → clear old grads (PyTorch ACCUMULATES otherwise)
   outputs = model(x)     → forward pass
   loss = criterion(out,y)  ← CrossEntropyLoss takes RAW LOGITS (no softmax)
   loss.backward()        → fresh gradients for this batch
   optimizer.step()       → update ALL learnable weights + biases
        • step wrapped in torch.no_grad(): the update is NOT tracked by autograd
        • plain-SGD step does exactly:  W ← W − lr · grad

HYPERPARAMETER SEARCH:
   Grid     → tries EVERY combo  (exhaustive; cost explodes with #params)
   Random   → samples combos     (wider coverage, fewer trials)
   Bayesian → uses past results  (fewest evals, heavier per iteration)

head: Search method
Trait
rows: Tests every combination — guaranteed coverage, expensive
Samples combos — more efficient, wider range in fewer trials
Models past results — fewest evaluations, complex / heavy per step

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Stochastic gradient descent: W ← W − η·∇W, b ← b − η·∇b. This is the rule a custom SGD's step() implements with param.data -= lr · param.grad.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Cost J(w) falling over iterations as the loop repeats. Batch GD uses the whole dataset per step — the trade-off the mini-batch and search choices try to manage.
